title: Adjusted R-Squared: The Real Score
concept: Adjusted R-squared measures a model's accuracy while penalizing the addition of useless features, ensuring the score only rises for genuinely valuable predictors.

exercises.prompt: Recap: If study hours has a coefficient of +3.0 and absences has a coefficient of -5.0, what is the change in predicted test score for 3 more study hours and 2 absences?

cards.title: Cheating the system
cards.body: Imagine a grade report that gives you extra points just for showing up with different colored pens. Standard R-squared behaves exactly like this: it rewards you just for adding features, even if they're useless.

cards.title: Adjusted R-Squared
cards.body: Adjusted R-squared is a modified version of R-squared that adjusts for the number of predictors in a model. It penalizes you for adding features that do not improve prediction accuracy.

cards.title: Packing for a hike
cards.body: Think of your model like a backpack. Adding gear (features) might help you, but each item adds weight. Standard R-squared only looks at utility. Adjusted R-squared subtracts points for the extra weight.

cards.title: The lottery ticket experiment
cards.body: If you predict home prices using size, your R-squared is 0.70. If you add 'random lottery numbers' as a feature, R-squared rises to 0.71. But Adjusted R-squared will drop to 0.68, flagging the useless addition.

cards.title: Always smaller than R-squared
cards.body: You might think Adjusted R-squared can be larger than regular R-squared. Actually, because of the penalty term, Adjusted R-squared is always less than or equal to R-squared.

cards.title: R-Squared vs. Adjusted R-Squared
cards.body: How they evaluate adding random inputs:

left.text:
Always goes up or stays same
Rewards adding random noise
Can lead to overfitting

right.text:
Goes down for useless features
Penalizes model complexity
Helps select best features

exercises.prompt: Why does raw (standard) R-squared always increase or stay the same when you add new features to a model?

options.text: Because mathematically, even random noise can slightly align with the target by pure chance.
Because the model automatically converts bad features into good ones.
Because adding features always reduces the size of the dataset.

exercises.explain: Correct! Adding any feature gives the model a tiny chance to fit random noise, which artificially inflates the standard R-squared score.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Adjusted R-squared can decrease when you add a new feature to the model.
exercises.explain: True. If the new feature does not improve the model's predictions enough to offset the penalty for complexity, Adjusted R-squared will drop.

exercises.sentence: Adjusted R-squared introduces a ___ for adding features that do not help the model.
exercises.explain: Adjusted R-squared penalizes the model for adding unnecessary variables, helping us avoid overfitting.

exercises.prompt: You add a student's favorite ice cream flavor to a model predicting their GPA. R-squared changes from 0.45 to 0.46. What is the most likely value of the Adjusted R-squared?
exercises.explain: Correct! Since ice cream flavor is unrelated to GPA, the penalty for adding a useless feature will cause the Adjusted R-squared to decrease.

exercises.prompt: You are comparing two models predicting company revenue. Model A uses 3 features (R-squared = 0.82, Adjusted R-squared = 0.81). Model B uses 15 features (R-squared = 0.84, Adjusted R-squared = 0.77). Which model should you choose?

options.text: Model A, because it has a higher Adjusted R-squared, indicating it achieves similar accuracy with far fewer useless features.
Model B, because its raw R-squared is higher, and we should always choose the model with the highest R-squared.
Model B, because having more features always makes a model more robust and interpretable.

exercises.explain: Spot on! Model A is simpler and more reliable. Adjusted R-squared correctly flags that Model B's extra 12 features are mostly adding noise rather than real predictive power, dragging down its adjusted score. Outstanding job completing the Multiple Linear Regression unit!
